claim: Always serialize timestamps in UTC to avoid cross-region comparison bugs
rationale: prior incidents with mixed local/UTC timestamps in order ledger

claim: Every must have explicit width and height attributes to keep CLS=0
rationale: layout stability convention in Sea Tree PWA — tested in Jan 2026 Lighthouse optimization pass

claim: Before a structural HTML change (lifting heading levels, renaming elements, changing tag names), grep the stylesheet for selectors that target the old element — update both HTML and CSS in the same pass.
rationale: On the WCAG pass for Sea Tree I ran 'replace_all <h4 → <h3' without checking styles.css, which targets .amenity-card h4, .bedroom-info h4, .footer h4, etc. The user reverted the HTML change to save styling. Loss = one round-trip + a partial commit that had to be unwound. Even simple find-replace on an HTML tag name is not safe without a CSS selector audit.

claim: Before trusting a commit's scope from its title, run 'git show --stat <sha>' — the title states intent, not actual changes. A small-sounding title with stats showing 1000+ line changes or touching files outside the title's scope signals a stealth refactor.
rationale: Commit b99796f on this repo was titled 'feat: sticky mobile booking bar' but its stat showed styles.css ±2530 lines and a 132-line index.html restructure — it was actually a full LUXE redesign merge that deleted the .animate-in CSS rule, leaving the site shipping with all 12 sections permanently invisible (opacity:0) for ~2 months. Nobody caught it because everyone trusted the commit title. The stat line would have flagged it in 5 seconds.

claim: When server-renders per-locale HTML, any DOMContentLoaded init that walks [data-translate] must seed from document.documentElement.lang — never hardcode a language string. Two writers to the same DOM state: the second one silently wins.
rationale: Sea Tree i18n bug (Apr 2026): build_locales.py pre-translated /es/, /el/, /fr/ HTML, but script.js ran setLanguage('en') on DOMContentLoaded and reverted every translated element back to English. The URL changed on click but the content snapped to EN, so the language switcher looked broken. Root cause is the hardcoded 'en', not the switcher.
